feedback.text: Greetings! As a citizen of Norway, I am still deeply affected by the digital fairness act and would like to let my concern regarding games and the lack of protection they have within the current legislation be heard. Preventing companies from revoking access to digital goods that have no expiration date would be a good start. This could be done by putting a law in place to enforce a publisher to have an end of life plan, in order to let the customer utilize the software they purchased, even after they stop supporting it directly. Even if all we get is the ability to engineer a solution ourselves, it would still be a huge step in the right direction. For more information on how to do it, I'd like to refer to the stop killing games campaigns FAQ. I'll even attach a link to the webpage for your convenience: www.stopkillinggames.com/faq Let this plea for reason be heard, so that we can one day see preservation be considered upon creation of a game, and the publishers right to destroy what has been purchased, revoked. May you have a wonderful day of smiting overzealous and exceedingly greedy corporations from upon high. Thank you very much for reading my thoughts on the matter! I hope to see a better tomorrow, where our art and culture in the form of games, gets the same recognition as film, music, literature, as well as other physical and digital artforms. Sincerely, J. Stegali.
